refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-003 Refined: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and tele-operators to achieve a measured energy efficiency of energy_per_ton ≤ E_target (customer-defined threshold) over the agreed evaluation period, through integrated monitoring, control optimization, and real-time feedback mechanisms.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is outcome-focused but lacks explicit connection to the mechanisms (monitoring, control, feedback) that enable efficiency achievement. Refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is not a passive property but an active capability requiring measurement, optimization logic, and operator awareness. This supports both direct operation and teleoperation modes.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure energy_per_ton = total_energy_consumed / total_tonnage_moved over a defined evaluation period (e.g., 8-hour shift, multi-shift campaign). Verify that measured value ≤ E_target. Collect telemetry from Vehicle power systems and cross-reference with load/distance data. Conduct acceptance testing under representative worksite conditions with Site Manager and Equipment Operator oversight.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log energy consumption (kWh or equivalent) from all propulsion and auxiliary systems.
CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log tonnage moved (or equivalent productivity metric) during operation.
CR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute and display energy_per_ton in real-time to the operator and tele-operator.
CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall provide control recommendations or automated optimization to reduce energy_per_ton below E_target.
CR-NGHE-003.5: The NGHE solution shall log all energy and productivity data for post-shift analysis and acceptance verification.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a centralized energy management system (EMS) that aggregates power telemetry from Vehicle subsystems, computes energy_per_ton in real-time, and broadcasts efficiency metrics via Telemetry interface to operator displays and tele-operator consoles; couple with predictive load-balancing algorithms to optimize power distribution.

DP-B: Distribute energy monitoring across Vehicle subsystems (motor controllers, auxiliary power units) with local efficiency computation; aggregate results at a central dashboard without predictive optimization.

DP-C: Implement energy efficiency as a passive logging function only; record energy and tonnage post-operation and compute energy_per_ton offline for acceptance verification, with no real-time feedback to operators.
DP-D: Integrate energy efficiency monitoring into Teleoperation module, allowing tele-operator to actively manage energy trade-offs via remote control of power modes and load distribution.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Implement a centralized energy management system (EMS) that aggregates power telemetry from Vehicle subsystems, computes energy_per_ton in real-time, and broadcasts efficiency metrics via Telemetry interface to operator displays and tele-operator consoles; couple with predictive load-balancing algorithms to optimize power distribution.

potential_sources: EMS depends on Vehicle power subsystems to provide accurate telemetry (power draw, voltage, current) – this is a necessary and well-defined dependency.
EMS must interface with operator display systems (in-cabin and remote) via Telemetry – standard interface, low coupling risk.
EMS optimization logic may influence Vehicle control decisions (e.g., motor speed, auxiliary load shedding) – this is intentional and managed via control feedback loops.
No circular dependencies detected; EMS is a consumer of Vehicle telemetry and a provider of efficiency metrics and control recommendations.
coupling_check.notes: DP-A exhibits acceptable coupling: it centralizes energy logic (high cohesion) and depends on well-defined external interfaces (Telemetry, Vehicle power data). The predictive optimization component introduces algorithmic complexity but does not create structural coupling issues if implemented as a stateless or loosely-coupled service.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Vehicle subsystems can provide real-time power telemetry with sufficient accuracy and frequency (e.g., ≥10 Hz sampling) to enable meaningful energy_per_ton computation.
Assumption: Operator and tele-operator displays can consume and render real-time efficiency metrics without latency degradation.
Risk: Predictive load-balancing algorithms may require tuning for different worksite conditions (terrain, load type, operator skill); incorrect tuning could degrade efficiency or safety.
Risk: Real-time computation of energy_per_ton requires accurate tonnage measurement; if tonnage sensor is unreliable, efficiency metric becomes unreliable.
Risk: High-frequency telemetry transmission may increase network bandwidth demand, impacting Teleoperation latency if network is constrained.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-A to explicitly specify: (1) telemetry sampling rate and accuracy requirements for power and tonnage sensors; (2) algorithm for energy_per_ton computation (e.g., rolling average window, outlier filtering); (3) fallback behavior if real-time computation fails (e.g., degrade to post-shift analysis); (4) tuning parameters for load-balancing algorithms and their adjustment mechanism (manual or adaptive).

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system integration boundary that orchestrates all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and provides the unified energy efficiency capability. The EMS (DP-A) is realized as a core service within NGHE Solution that aggregates telemetry from Vehicle, computes efficiency metrics, and distributes results via Telemetry interface to operator interfaces and tele-operator consoles.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives power consumption, tonnage, and operational state data from Vehicle subsystems via Telemetry (inbound). NGHE Solution transmits computed energy_per_ton, efficiency recommendations, and control optimization signals via Telemetry (outbound) to operator displays and tele-operator consoles.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives operational tasks and mode commands (e.g., 'move load X to location Y', 'optimize for energy', 'prioritize speed') via Tasking interface from Site Manager or tele-operator, which inform energy optimization strategy.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution may transmit safety-critical alerts (e.g., 'energy efficiency optimization disabled due to safety constraint') via SafetyAlert interface to Safety Services and operator displays.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution uses Network Link to transmit telemetry and receive commands in tele-operated scenarios; energy efficiency metrics are included in telemetry payload.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level boundary for energy efficiency orchestration. EMS logic (DP-A) is best realized as a core service within NGHE Solution because it must integrate telemetry from multiple subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and distribute results system-wide. Containment is high; diffusion risk is minimal (only the Telemetry interface boundary is external).

Vehicle could host local energy monitoring (e.g., motor controller efficiency tracking), but centralizing EMS in NGHE Solution avoids duplicating efficiency logic across Vehicle subsystems and enables cross-subsystem optimization. If EMS were placed in Vehicle, it would need to expose efficiency metrics via Telemetry, creating diffusion of energy logic across module boundaries.

Teleoperation could include energy efficiency features (e.g., tele-operator-driven power mode selection), but the core EMS (measurement, computation, optimization) should remain in NGHE Solution to avoid coupling energy logic to teleoperation-specific concerns. Teleoperation should consume efficiency metrics from NGHE Solution via Telemetry.

Safety Services should not contain the primary EMS logic. However, Safety Services may enforce constraints on energy optimization (e.g., 'do not reduce power to safety-critical systems to improve efficiency') and receive SafetyAlert notifications from NGHE Solution if optimization is disabled.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The energy efficiency capability (DP-A: centralized EMS) is best contained within NGHE Solution because: (1) it requires aggregation of telemetry from multiple subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), which is a system-level concern; (2) it must distribute efficiency metrics and control recommendations to multiple consumers (operator displays, tele-operator consoles, Vehicle control systems), which is a system-level orchestration task; (3) centralizing EMS avoids algorithmic duplication and ensures consistent efficiency computation across all operational modes (direct operation, teleoperation, autonomous).
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution becomes a monolithic module, EMS logic may be difficult to test, version, and maintain independently. Mitigation: Implement EMS as a well-defined microservice or plugin within NGHE Solution with clear interfaces.

Risk: Real-time computation of energy_per_ton in NGHE Solution may introduce latency if the module is overloaded with other system-level tasks. Mitigation: Profile performance; consider offloading computation to a dedicated energy management service or Vehicle subsystem if latency becomes critical.

Risk: If Vehicle subsystems do not provide timely or accurate telemetry, NGHE Solution cannot compute reliable efficiency metrics. Mitigation: Define telemetry SLAs (sampling rate, accuracy, latency) in Vehicle module specification; implement fallback logic in NGHE Solution if telemetry is degraded.

Risk: Tele-operator may not have sufficient visibility into energy efficiency trade-offs (e.g., 'reducing power improves efficiency but increases task duration'). Mitigation: Provide decision-support features in Teleoperation module that highlight efficiency implications of operator commands.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Specify NGHE Solution module to include: (1) Energy Management Service (EMS) as a distinct logical component with defined inputs (Vehicle telemetry, Tasking commands, Safety constraints) and outputs (efficiency metrics, control recommendations, SafetyAlerts); (2) Telemetry aggregation layer that collects power and tonnage data from Vehicle and distributes efficiency metrics to operator interfaces; (3) Configuration interface for tuning EMS parameters (e.g., optimization algorithm weights, fallback thresholds); (4) Logging and audit trail for all energy efficiency decisions and metrics for post-shift analysis and acceptance verification.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (centralized EMS with real-time computation and optimization) directly addresses the refined FR (enable achievement of energy_per_ton ≤ E_target). The DP provides mechanisms for measurement, computation, feedback, and optimization that are necessary and sufficient to support the FR. No logical gaps detected.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: If EMS telemetry aggregation logic is distributed across NGHE Solution and Vehicle modules, efficiency computation becomes fragmented. Mitigation: Centralize all aggregation logic in NGHE Solution; Vehicle provides raw telemetry only.

Diffusion Risk 2: If operator display logic for rendering efficiency metrics is duplicated in both in-cabin and tele-operator consoles, changes to efficiency computation require updates in multiple places. Mitigation: Implement efficiency metric rendering as a shared component or service consumed by both display systems.

Diffusion Risk 3: If load-balancing optimization algorithms are implemented in both NGHE Solution and Vehicle control systems, they may conflict or produce inconsistent results. Mitigation: Implement optimization logic in NGHE Solution only; Vehicle control systems execute recommendations from NGHE Solution.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary for DP-A. The module has sufficient scope to contain EMS logic, aggregation, and distribution without requiring external dependencies beyond well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link). No evidence of over-containment (module too large) or under-containment (logic split across multiple modules).

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of CR-NGHE-003 is feasible via acceptance testing: (1) Instrument Vehicle with power and tonnage sensors; (2) Operate NGHE Solution under representative worksite conditions; (3) Collect telemetry from NGHE Solution (energy_per_ton metrics); (4) Compare measured energy_per_ton against E_target over agreed evaluation period; (5) Verify that NGHE Solution correctly computes and logs efficiency metrics. Verification is straightforward and does not require specialized test equipment beyond standard power and load measurement tools.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of CR-NGHE-003 to NGHE Solution is clear. The module is responsible for: (1) aggregating telemetry from Vehicle; (2) computing energy_per_ton; (3) providing real-time feedback to operators; (4) optimizing power distribution; (5) logging data for acceptance verification. All sub-FRs (CR-NGHE-003.1 through CR-NGHE-003.5) are allocated to NGHE Solution with clear ownership.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Specify telemetry SLAs (sampling rate, accuracy, latency) for power and tonnage sensors in Vehicle module to ensure NGHE Solution can compute reliable energy_per_ton metrics.

Revision 2: Define energy_per_ton computation algorithm (e.g., rolling average window, outlier filtering, units) in NGHE Solution specification to ensure consistent interpretation across all stakeholders.
Revision 3: Specify fallback behavior for NGHE Solution if real-time computation fails (e.g., degrade to post-shift analysis, alert operator, disable optimization) to ensure safety and reliability.

Revision 4: Define tuning parameters for load-balancing optimization algorithms and their adjustment mechanism (manual by Site Manager, adaptive based on worksite conditions, or hybrid) in NGHE Solution specification.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-003 is not atomic; it comprises multiple sub-functions (measurement, computation, feedback, optimization, logging) that require further decomposition. The refined FR and selected DP are sound, and the IM assignment to NGHE Solution is appropriate. However, to ensure complete and verifiable design, each sub-FR (CR-NGHE-003.1 through CR-NGHE-003.5) must be decomposed into its own FR-DP-IM cycle. This will clarify ownership of measurement logic (Vehicle vs. NGHE Solution), computation algorithms (NGHE Solution), feedback mechanisms (operator interfaces), and logging (data persistence layer).
decision.justification_detail: The gate review identified no blocking issues, but recommended revisions indicate that further specification is needed at the next decomposition level. Specifically: (1) telemetry SLAs must be defined for Vehicle subsystems; (2) energy_per_ton computation algorithm must be specified; (3) fallback behavior must be designed; (4) optimization algorithm tuning must be addressed. These are best handled by decomposing each sub-FR into its own FR-DP-IM cycle, which will produce detailed specifications for each component.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log energy consumption (kWh or equivalent) from all propulsion and auxiliary systems.
CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log tonnage moved (or equivalent productivity metric) during operation.
CR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute and display energy_per_ton in real-time to the operator and tele-operator.
CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall provide control recommendations or automated optimization to reduce energy_per_ton below E_target.
CR-NGHE-003.5: The NGHE solution shall log all energy and productivity data for post-shift analysis and acceptance verification.

assumptions: Assumption 1: Vehicle subsystems can provide real-time power telemetry with sufficient accuracy (±5% or better) and frequency (≥10 Hz) to enable meaningful energy_per_ton computation.
Assumption 2: Tonnage measurement is available from Vehicle load sensors or external weighing systems with accuracy ±10% or better.
Assumption 3: Operator and tele-operator displays can consume and render real-time efficiency metrics without introducing latency >500 ms.
Assumption 4: Network bandwidth is sufficient to transmit high-frequency telemetry (power, tonnage, efficiency metrics) without degrading Teleoperation control latency.
Assumption 5: Site Manager will define E_target (energy efficiency threshold) prior to acceptance testing; E_target is achievable with the selected DP (centralized EMS with optimization).
Assumption 6: Worksite conditions during acceptance testing are representative of normal operating conditions; energy_per_ton measured during testing is predictive of field performance.
open_risks: Risk 1 (High): Predictive load-balancing algorithms may require extensive tuning for different worksite conditions (terrain, load type, operator skill); incorrect tuning could degrade efficiency or safety. Mitigation: Conduct algorithm validation testing across representative worksite scenarios; provide Site Manager with tuning guidance.

Risk 2 (High): Real-time computation of energy_per_ton requires accurate tonnage measurement; if tonnage sensor is unreliable, efficiency metric becomes unreliable and acceptance verification fails. Mitigation: Specify tonnage sensor accuracy requirements in Vehicle module; conduct sensor validation testing.

Risk 3 (Medium): High-frequency telemetry transmission may increase network bandwidth demand, impacting Teleoperation latency if network is constrained. Mitigation: Profile network usage; implement telemetry compression or adaptive sampling if bandwidth is insufficient.

Risk 4 (Medium): NGHE Solution may become a performance bottleneck if EMS computation is not optimized; real-time efficiency metrics may lag behind actual operations. Mitigation: Profile EMS performance; consider offloading computation to Vehicle subsystems or dedicated hardware if latency becomes critical.

Risk 5 (Low): Operator may misinterpret energy_per_ton metric or make suboptimal decisions based on real-time feedback. Mitigation: Provide operator training and decision-support features (e.g., 'current efficiency is 10% above target; recommend reducing speed by 5%').
evidence_needed: Evidence 1: Telemetry SLA specification for Vehicle power and tonnage sensors (sampling rate, accuracy, latency).
Evidence 2: Energy_per_ton computation algorithm specification (formula, rolling average window, outlier filtering, units).
Evidence 3: Load-balancing optimization algorithm specification (objective function, constraints, tuning parameters).
Evidence 4: Fallback behavior specification for NGHE Solution if real-time computation fails (e.g., degrade to post-shift analysis, alert operator, disable optimization).
Evidence 5: Operator interface specification for displaying energy_per_ton and efficiency recommendations (in-cabin display, tele-operator console).
Evidence 6: Acceptance test plan for CR-NGHE-003 (test scenarios, measurement methodology, pass/fail criteria).
Evidence 7: Network bandwidth analysis for telemetry transmission (data rate, latency impact on Teleoperation).
Evidence 8: Algorithm validation testing results across representative worksite scenarios (terrain, load type, operator skill).
